specific_location: Small pond adjacent to Prewitt Res
description: Around 3:30 on the afternoon of 11 September 2011, Tracy Clark and I were walking around a willow and cottonwood fringed pond adjacent to Prewitt Reservoir in Washington County. A bird flushed in front of us, landing at about eye level, about 20 feet away (perhaps as close as 15 feet). Bright shade was the lighting conditions, and I was using Swarovski EL 10x42 bins. The bird remained stationary for a couple minutes, then slowly slinked through the tree, eventually giving us excellent views of undertail before taking off and disappearing. Total time of view 5+ minutes. My camera had broken during a fall earlier in the day, alas.  The bird was long and slim, typical for a cuckoo (Yellow-billed/Black-billed) in shape and size, perhaps appearing a bit smaller/less bulky than I'd expect from a YB Cuckoo. The head was the first part of the bird observed in detail. The bill was blackish, slightly decurved and a bit blunt shaped, giving the appearance (with some creativity) of a dark gray, somewhat straight, banana. There was no yellow in the bill. The eye was dark and was surrounded by a pale amber-yellow orbital ring. The forehead and crown were brown washed with gray fading into a more pure brown somewhere between mid-crown and nape. There was a faint hint of a duskier/darker facial mask. The throat and upper chest were pale dingy gray, forming a bibbed appearance. There was a fairly distinct demarcation between this and the remainder of the underparts which were dingy white washed in a fawn-buff. The tail was quite worn, and from beneath, appeared dull silvery-gray without any markings. The back and wings were dull brown, lacking any rust tones at all, while perched or after taking flight.Behaviors: see descriptionCall: not heardPlumage: juvenile
